core_ideas: Objects that make contact with each other apply equally strong forces on each other in opposite directions.
Objects that collide apply an equally strong peak force (maximum force) on each other during the collision.
Increasing the speed or mass of a moving object increases its kinetic energy (KE).
The more KE that objects in a system have, the higher the peak forces they can produce in a collision. (It is right only when the time is controlled; like the collision is an 'immediate')

misconceptions.1-a: Heavier object exerts the larger force in a collision.
misconceptions.1-b: Faster object exerts the larger force.
misconceptions.1-c: The 'active' pusher produces more force than the 'passive' object.
misconceptions.2-a: At the moment of impact, the more massive object exerts the larger peak force.
misconceptions.3-a: Increasing KE of an object indicates a larger force applied to the object.
misconceptions.4-a: During a collision, an object with more KE applies a larger peak force than that it receives.

quiz_items.stem: A large delivery truck collides with a small mosquito. During the brief time they are in contact, how do the forces they exert on each other compare?

options.A: They exert forces of equal strength on each other.
options.B: The truck exerts a stronger force on the mosquito.
options.C: The mosquito exerts a stronger force on the truck.
options.D: Neither exerts a force because the mosquito is too small.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?

A soccer player (Player A) collides with a stationary player (Player B). In a second trial, Player A runs twice as fast before hitting Player B. In both trials, the collision lasts for the same amount of time. Which statement best explains the forces involved?

options.A: Player A exerts a larger force than Player B in the second collision.
options.B: The peak force is the same in both collisions because Player B is standing still.
options.C: The peak force is larger in the second collision because Player A has more kinetic energy.
options.D: The peak force is smaller in the second collision because faster motion reduces contact time.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
Two students stand on skateboards facing each other. Student A pushes on Student B's hands to start B rolling away. While their hands are in contact, how do the forces compare?

options.A: Student B exerts no force because B is passive.
options.B: Student B exerts a larger force because B is being pushed backward.
options.C: Student A exerts a larger force because A is the one doing the pushing.
options.D: They exert forces of equal strength on each other in opposite directions.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?

A moving hockey puck hits a stationary puck of the same mass in a very brief collision. The moving puck has more kinetic energy before impact. During the collision, how do the peak forces on the two pucks compare?

options.A: The peak forces are equal in strength on both pucks.
options.B: The stationary puck exerts a larger peak force because it is not moving.
options.C: Only the moving puck exerts a force because the other puck is stationary.
options.D: The moving puck exerts a larger peak force because it has more kinetic energy.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
